$schema_note: Misconception suggestions harvested by MisconceptionAnalyst from common_miss prose in data/buckets/verb_form.passato_prossimo.json. PENDING architecture ratification into data/misconceptions.json. Governance mirrors bucket_suggestions_<topic>.json. Thread: inter_chat/Architecture_MisconceptionAnalyst_registry_harvest.md.

proposed_family_actions.note: auxiliary_choice is a reserved cross-tense family with no specifics seeded. The passato prossimo work gives it its first foothold, as DESIGN §15 anticipated.

proposed_misconceptions.proposed_label: avere used where essere is required
proposed_misconceptions.proposed_description: Selecting avere as the compound-tense auxiliary for a verb that takes essere: motion/change intransitives, reflexives, and the piacere family.
examples: Mi ho lavato (for mi sono lavato)
Le ha piaciuto (for le è piaciuto)
proposed_misconceptions.cross_kind_note: CROSS-TENSE and CROSS-KIND: harvested from both the passato tree and the pronoun tree (reflexives, piacere). The default-avere over-extension recurs in every compound tense.
proposed_misconceptions.rationale: The dominant auxiliary error. Default-avere over-application; the reflexive and piacere cases are sub-instances, not separate misconceptions.

proposed_misconceptions.proposed_label: essere used where avere is required
proposed_misconceptions.proposed_description: Selecting essere for a transitive or avere-taking verb, the rarer reverse of the default-avere error, often a hyper-correction.
examples: sono mangiato la pasta (for ho mangiato la pasta)
proposed_misconceptions.rationale: Kept as its own specific because direction matters analytically: over-using essere is a different learner state from defaulting to avere.

proposed_misconceptions.proposed_description: Forming an irregular past participle by the regular -ato/-uto/-ito rule instead of the suppletive/contracted form.
proposed_misconceptions.cross_kind_note: CROSS-TENSE sibling of regularisation.irregular_verb: the same regularising impulse, on participles. Recurs in every compound tense.
proposed_misconceptions.rationale: One misconception over many verbs (fato, dito, prenduto all point up to it), exactly the surface-to-pattern collapse DESIGN §15 wants.

proposed_misconceptions.proposed_label: essere-participle fails to agree with the subject
proposed_misconceptions.proposed_description: With essere as auxiliary, leaving the participle in the masculine-singular default instead of agreeing in gender and number with the subject.
examples: sono andati (for a feminine group, sono andate)
proposed_misconceptions.cross_kind_note: CROSS-KIND agreement: same agreement engine as adjective-noun agreement; the participle behaves like a predicate adjective.
proposed_misconceptions.rationale: Core participle-agreement miss on the essere side.

proposed_misconceptions.proposed_label: avere-participle fails to agree with a preceding direct-object clitic
proposed_misconceptions.proposed_description: With avere as auxiliary and a preceding 3rd-person DOP (lo/la/li/le) or partitive ne, leaving the participle invariable instead of agreeing with that object.
examples: le ho visti / le ho visto (for le ho viste)
ne ho mangiato tre (for ne ho mangiate tre)
proposed_misconceptions.cross_kind_note: CROSS-KIND: harvested from both the passato tree and the pronoun tree (ne, left-dislocation). The 1st/2nd-person optional case (preceding_dop_mi_ti_ci_vi) should NOT be tagged when the invariable form is the everyday default.
proposed_misconceptions.rationale: Single agreement pattern straddling the verb and pronoun trees; a clean cross-kind exemplar.

proposed_misconceptions.proposed_label: avere-participle wrongly agreed with the subject
proposed_misconceptions.proposed_description: Agreeing the participle with the subject when avere is the auxiliary and there is no preceding DOP, where it must stay invariable. The over-application (hyper-correction) error.
examples: ho mangiata (for ho mangiato, female speaker)
proposed_misconceptions.cross_kind_note: Inverse of agreement.participle_preceding_dop: agreement supplied where it is barred. Mirrors regularisation.invariable_adjective's over-application shape.
proposed_misconceptions.rationale: Distinct learner state: knows agreement exists, applies it too widely. Worth its own tag for the drill-down.

proposed_misconceptions.proposed_label: Auxiliary in the wrong person/number for the subject
proposed_misconceptions.proposed_description: Conjugating the compound-tense auxiliary for the wrong person or number relative to the subject (subject-verb agreement failure on the auxiliary).
examples: Marco hanno parlato (for Marco ha parlato)
proposed_misconceptions.cross_kind_note: CROSS-KIND subject-verb agreement: same shape as person errors in simple tenses and the seed agreement.reflexive_pronoun_person.
proposed_misconceptions.rationale: A subject-verb agreement error that lands on the auxiliary; belongs with agreement, not auxiliary_choice (which is about essere-vs-avere, not person).

proposed_misconceptions.proposed_label: Adverb or negation misplaced around aux + participle
proposed_misconceptions.proposed_description: Breaking the fixed aux-then-participle block: putting negation or a frequency/time adverb (già, ancora, mai, sempre, appena) outside the slot between auxiliary and participle.
examples: ho non mangiato (for non ho mangiato)
ho mangiato già (for ho già mangiato)
proposed_misconceptions.cross_kind_note: word_order family (alongside adjective and clitic position). The negation case overlaps the proposed transfer family; candidate co-tag.
proposed_misconceptions.rationale: Both misses are the same structural fact: the aux+participle is a unit and the adverb/negator sits inside it.

cross_referenced_into_other_trees.note: Marco e Anna sono andate (for andati) is the SAME last-noun-attraction misconception as the adjective italiane/italiani case. One shared cross-kind specific, tagged from both trees.
